_provenance.note: Web-research → adversarial-verify → rubric-score; cited + confidence-scored. Enriched 2026-06-20 with magazine tests (Yachting Monthly, boats.com), Amel Yacht School buying guide, Amel Yacht Owners community, and brokerage pricing.

facts.value: Category A (Ocean) - the highest EU Recreational Craft Directive classification, intended for extended offshore passages in winds above Beaufort Force 8 and significant wave heights well above 4m. Designed by Berret-Racoupeau and built by Amel from roughly 2011 to 2018 as the last of the classic Amel ketches before the sloop-rigged Amel 50.

5,900 kg (13,007 lbs) cast iron keel, L-shaped with a bulb, giving a 27% ballast-to-displacement ratio. The keel is unusual: the propeller shaft runs through the elongated cast-iron keel, so the three-blade folding prop sits at the keel's trailing edge well forward of the rudder. This eliminates prop-walk but makes the boat slew when going astern.

Capsize Screening Formula 1.75 (under 2.0 is desirable for offshore work), Comfort Ratio 38.66 (firmly in the moderate-to-heavy bluewater range), and SA/D ratio 15.58 (lightly canvased, confirming the boat's deliberately sedate, comfort-first character rather than performance). No published AVS or STIX number exists for the boat.

Hydronic diesel furnace providing distributed warm-air heating throughout the boat, plus optional 220V Climma reverse-cycle air conditioning that also delivers heat to individual cabins and the saloon. Amel's design brief explicitly keeps cabin comfort high in cold climates, fitting heating, dishwasher and microwave as part of the standard liveaboard package.

Hull is solid GRP below the waterline with a balsa-sandwich deck and coachroof; the boat is heavily insulated and sound-deadened, which keeps interior temperatures and noise levels comfortable. Exact insulation thickness is not published, and the balsa-cored deck is the structure most at risk of moisture ingress if deck fittings leak.

Both masts are deck-stepped, not keel-stepped. Compression loads are carried into the hull by laminated support pillars and chainplate tie-rods bonded to the structure, with two levels of swept-back spreaders. Because the masts do not penetrate the cabin, there is no keel-stepped cold bridge through the interior.

Four watertight bulkheads divide the hull, separating the accommodation from the bow locker and the lazarette and isolating major compartments. This is a genuine collision and flooding safeguard and a long-standing Amel hallmark. (An earlier claim of five compartments and two bulkheads was traced to the different Amel 50 and corrected.)

Deep centre cockpit with a sheltered steering position, enclosed by either a canvas-top Cabriolet or a GRP hardtop. The hardtop version is the one to look for on a world-cruising boat: it keeps the helm and the whole cockpit dry and protected in cold or wet weather. Sail handling is done from the helm via electric furlers and winches, so the crew stays under cover.

Numerous opening hatches, portlights and hull windows give good cross-flow, and an electric extractor hood ventilates the galley. The enclosed cockpit can be opened up for airflow in port. Some specific window-count claims could not be verified, but the general standard of natural ventilation is well regarded.

The enclosed cockpit (Cabriolet canvas or GRP hardtop) provides full sun and rain protection at the helm and over the cockpit table. The hardtop is the recommended option for tropical cruising because it shades the cockpit permanently without the maintenance and UV degradation of canvas.

Generous cold storage as standard: multiple fridges plus freezer capacity, with front-loading and drawer-style units and a large freezer in the saloon area depending on configuration. Exact volumes vary by hull and were not reliably documented, but the boat is fitted for long tropical provisioning runs without resupply.

Dessalator watermaker fitted as standard; the boats.com test and the C'LUV listing both cite roughly 240-250 L/h capacity (an earlier 160 L/h figure was incorrect). The 220V unit runs off the generator or inverter and, combined with 800L water tankage, gives a couple effectively unlimited fresh water offshore.

Passive airflow comes from hatches, hull windows and the openable cockpit; active cooling is the optional Climma reverse-cycle air conditioning, which most tropical-bound owners fit. The heavy refrigeration and AC load is a real draw on the electrical system and is one reason the boat carries a large battery bank, generator and watermaker.

900 litres fuel and 800 litres water as standard. With the 110hp Volvo D3 sipping fuel at displacement speeds, that fuel load gives a long motoring range for calms and canal transits, and the 800L water tank plus the standard Dessalator watermaker makes the boat self-sufficient for a couple on extended passages.

Hydraulic Furuno Navpilot 711 C autopilot standard, often fitted as twin independent units for redundancy on the better-equipped hulls. The 24V system and highly automated electric sail controls mean the boat is designed to be sailed almost entirely on autopilot - the Yachting Monthly test noted she spends 99 per cent of the time under autopilot.

No factory windvane option. The boat relies entirely on its electric/hydraulic autopilots, which suits the all-electric sail-handling concept but means there is no mechanical self-steering fallback if the electrical system fails. A servo-pendulum windvane is theoretically retrofittable to the transom but is not something Amel configures, and no source confirms a clean installation.

Dual 24V DC / 220V AC system with a house bank around 600 Ah at 24V (six 200Ah batteries typical), Mastervolt 2500W and 2000W inverters, an Onan 7.5kW diesel generator, and 400W or more of solar depending on hull. It is enough to run the watermaker, autopilot, electric furlers and full electronics offshore, but the heavy AC/refrigeration load means the generator gets regular use.

Large folding/swim platform at the transom giving easy dinghy and water access, a cavernous lazarette, and a self-bailing cockpit. The transom design is built around easy boarding and tender handling rather than stern MOB recovery in a seaway.

Moderate freeboard with a deep centre cockpit set well into the hull, prioritising a sheltered, low helm position and comfortable motion over a high topsides look. Exact freeboard figures are not published.

Moulded non-slip side decks (no teak to replace) with tall, solid stainless guardrails running bow to stern and substantial handrails, so the crew can move forward securely in a seaway. Storage lockers sit beneath the side decks. The all-stainless guardrail and rail-mounted handhold layout is a deliberate offshore-safety feature.

A Jon Buoy MOB recovery module is documented on at least one boat (C'LUV), and the skeg-hung rudder plus solid handrails support crew safety. Realistically, recovering a casualty over the high topsides of a 24-tonne ketch shorthanded is hard, so the practical emphasis is on staying aboard - jacklines, tethers and the enclosed cockpit.

No first-hand published storm log specific to the Amel 55 was located. What is consistently reported is the design intent: a heavy 24-tonne displacement, 27% ballast ratio, Category A certification, deeply sheltered enclosed cockpit, and a ketch rig that lets the boat run comfortably under reefed mizzen and staysail alone when conditions deteriorate. The flip side is that the boat's heavy-weather security depends on the electric furlers working - a furler that jams in a rising gale is the boat's real heavy-weather risk.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors

facts.value: Cutter-headed ketch: mainsail roughly 47 m², genoa roughly 47 m², mizzen roughly 29 m², plus a 22 m² staysail option, all on electric furlers with manual overrides. Because the sail area is split across four sails, no single sail is unmanageable, and the boat can be progressively de-powered to mizzen-and-staysail for heavy weather. Henri Amel's rule of thumb was that anyone who could lift 30 lb could handle the boat.

Weak astern handling is a known characteristic. The prop sits at the trailing edge of the keel, a long way forward of the skeg-hung rudder, so the boat has almost no prop-walk but slews off line as it gathers sternway and water flow forces the big rudder hard over. The standard technique is to centre the rudder and steer astern on throttle and the standard 12hp retractable bow thruster. Close-quarters manoeuvring is described as sluggish, and the bow thruster is considered an essential tool, not a luxury.

Volvo Penta D3-110: a five-cylinder, 2.4-litre, 110hp common-rail diesel with the shaft drive integrated into the cast-iron keel. It is a compact, modern engine sitting in an unusually accessible walk-in engine room reached by lifting the cockpit sole on gas struts. The earlier '144 HP' figure in this record was wrong - both the boats.com and TradeABoat tests confirm 110hp.

Single-strand stainless standing rigging with the masts deck-stepped onto laminated support pillars and chainplate tie-rods bonded into the hull. Industry practice is a 10-15 year offshore re-rig and a rig pull every 5-6 years to inspect tangs, chainplates, turnbuckles and mast bases. With the youngest hulls now around 2018 and the oldest around 2011, most boats are at or past first re-rig, and the ketch carries roughly 40% more rigging than a comparable sloop.

The buyer's checklist is well documented. (1) Electric in-mast and genoa furlers reach end of service life and can hesitate or jam - a safety-critical and expensive failure; the manual override on the main works by unbolting two fixings but seizes from corrosion if neglected. (2) Steering U-joints wear through invisibly under the enclosed cockpit, signalled only by stiffness or a metallic grinding, and access requires cockpit disassembly. (3) Balsa-cored deck and coachroof can take on moisture around leaking fittings - flagged as a potential six-figure problem. (4) Rudder play and skeg cracking. (5) Volvo D3 overheating under load is usually the start of a costly engine chapter. (6) C-drive mechanical seals can let seawater mix with the drive oil, and improvised foam-sealed repairs are documented and must be corrected. (7) Electric winches, generator and complex 24V electrical system are excellent maintained but expensive once neglected.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors

facts.value: Used Amel 55s (build years roughly 2011-2017) trade broadly between about $540,000 and $865,000, or roughly 620,000-790,000 euros in Europe, with a clear age/condition gradient: a 2012 hull around $750,000 and a 2015 around $835,000 are representative brokerage asks. Condition matters enormously - a documented, well-serviced boat sits near the top of the range while a neglected example with furler, steering, C-drive or deck-core problems can need a six-figure refit on top of a lower purchase price.

Parts and support are good for a niche brand: the Volvo Penta D3-110 and Onan 7.5kW generator are mainstream, Furuno electronics are widely serviceable, and Amel minimised through-hulls with a single inlet and seawater manifold. The Amel-specific items - Reckmann furler motors and gearboxes, the C-drive, and the bespoke 24V architecture - need specialist dealers (Swiftsure, Ancasta, the French Amel network) but are well supported by the 1,000-plus-member Amel Yacht Owners community and the Amel Yacht School. (A previously cited Wilkinson Marine dealer link could not be verified.)

praise: Commitment to detail and quality construction unmatched in class
Exceptional stowage space and innovative storage features throughout
Comfort, reliability, and ease of operation - minimum effort for shorthanded sailing
Practical design philosophy with manual backups for all critical systems
Fabulously comfortable, almost regal progress at sea
Highly automated with binnacle controls - five-minute solo sail deployment
Outstanding build quality - 25 years of design evolution in the Amel 54/55 lineage
Everything has a manual override - the boat is not crippled if the electronics fail
Excellent seaworthiness for ocean cruising; Category A certified
Deep, fully enclosed centre cockpit keeps the crew protected in cold and wet weather
Walk-in engine room with standing access to engine and generator - rare at this size
Four watertight bulkheads provide genuine collision and flooding protection
Strong community support with 1,000+ Amel Yacht Owners members providing peer advice
complaints: Notably expensive to buy and to maintain compared to alternatives
Heavy, dull helm when overcanvassed, particularly with too much mizzen
Sedate performance - deliberately not quick, with a low SA/D ratio
Weak astern handling: slews off line, near-total reliance on the bow thruster for close quarters
Electric furlers reach end of service life and are expensive and dangerous to fix
Steering U-joint wear hidden under the enclosed cockpit, hard to detect on a viewing
Balsa-cored deck can suffer moisture ingress - potentially a six-figure problem
Complex 24V electrical system with heavy AC/refrigeration load leans on the generator
C-drive mechanical seals can let seawater into the drive oil
No mechanical self-steering fallback - autopilot-only, no factory windvane
known_issues: Electric furling systems on both masts - safety-critical; failure is expensive and dangerous
Manual furler override seizes from corrosion if not regularly exercised
Steering system U-joint wear - hard to detect, access needs cockpit disassembly
Balsa deck-core moisture in coachroof and around fittings
Rudder play and skeg cracking from age or grounding
Volvo D3-110 overheating under sustained load
C-drive mechanical seal failure - seawater mixing with drive oil
Improvised foam-sealed repairs around the C-drive bushing documented and need correcting
Electric winch, generator and electrical system neglect costly to put right
Astern handling and close-quarters manoeuvring sluggish without active bow-thruster use

forums.title: Amel lost its mast - halyard wrap discussion - AmelYachtOwners
YBW Forum - Amel rigging and maintenance discussions

refit_items.system: Standing Rigging (both masts, if original/10+ years)
refit_items.action: Complete re-rig of both masts: shrouds, stays, tie-rods inspection, turnbuckles and associated hardware, plus pulling the rig to check mast bases and chainplates. The ketch carries roughly 40% more rigging than a comparable sloop.
Replace the full four-sail suit - mainsail, genoa, staysail and mizzen - in furling-compatible profiles to suit the Reckmann systems. A large, premium suit for a furling ketch of this size.

Overhaul or replace the electric furler motors and gearboxes on the in-mast mainsail and the genoa - the boat's most safety-critical known issue. Includes freeing and servicing the manual overrides so they actually work in an emergency. Inspect under load during sea trial.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors
Rebuilding the In-Mast Furling System on an Amel - YouTube

refit_items.action: Replace worn steering U-joints and inspect the full linkage, quadrant and cables. Triggered by stiffness or a metallic grinding; access requires partial cockpit disassembly. A near-certainty on older or hard-used hulls.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors

refit_items.action: Replace the C-drive mechanical seals if there is any sign of seawater in the drive oil or weeping, and properly remediate any improvised foam-sealed repairs with correct hardware. Seal kit plus specialist labour.

Comprehensive service - impeller, heat exchanger, zincs, thermostat, injectors, full fluids - escalating to overhaul if hours are high or the engine overheats under load. The D3 is a modern common-rail engine; chronic overheating signals a costly chapter.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors

refit_items.action: Service or overhaul the generator - capacitors, brushes, impeller, cooling circuit - and address any vibration or cooling history. Generator neglect is common on brokerage boats and the boat leans on it for AC, watermaker and charging.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors

refit_items.action: Remediate moisture in the balsa-cored deck and coachroof - localised epoxy treatment for isolated wet areas up to full core replacement of the coachroof. A moisture-meter survey of all deck and coachroof areas is mandatory before purchase; this item can dwarf every other on the list.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors

refit_items.action: Replace rudder bearings if play is detected and repair any skeg cracking by epoxy injection or rebuild. Check for grounding history on the encapsulated keel.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors

refit_items.action: Replace the ~600Ah-at-24V house bank like-for-like in AGM, or upgrade to LiFePO4 to make better use of the Mastervolt inverter infrastructure and reduce generator hours. All hulls are past the AGM service interval by 2026.

Service the electric primary and sheet winches, inspect the complex 24V wiring, breakers and bus bars, and refresh inverter/charger contactors and fuses. Excellent when maintained, expensive once neglected.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors

refit_items.action: Inspect and partially replace seacocks and re-bed through-hulls. Amel's single-inlet seawater manifold reduces the count of fittings, but those present should be pressure-tested and serviced at this age.

provisioning_specific.item: Reckmann electric furler motor brushes, gearbox seals and grease (both masts)
provisioning_specific.why: Electric furling hesitation is the boat's most dangerous and expensive known failure. A furler that will not furl in a rising gale is an offshore emergency. Carry brushes, seals and the correct lubricant for both the in-mast main and the genoa Reckmann units, and keep the manual overrides exercised so they free off when needed.

provisioning_specific.item: C-drive mechanical seal kit and spare C-drive oil
provisioning_specific.why: Seawater-into-oil mixing in the C-drive is documented with photographic evidence. A seal failure offshore needs immediate attention; carrying the seal kit and correct oil grade avoids a haul-out diversion.

provisioning_specific.item: Steering U-joint replacement assembly
provisioning_specific.why: U-joint wear is a common failure that hides under the enclosed cockpit and announces itself as stiffness or grinding. A spare assembly turns an offshore failure into an at-anchor repair rather than a tow.

provisioning_specific.item: Hydraulic fluid, filters and bleed kit for the Furuno autopilot circuit
provisioning_specific.why: The boat is autopilot-only with no windvane fallback, and the twin Furuno Navpilot units share a hydraulic circuit - a leak or pump fault can disable both. Carry fluid and know the bleed procedure so steering stays automated on long shorthanded passages.

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo Penta D3-110 raw-water impeller, thermostat and heat-exchanger zinc kit
provisioning_specific.why: Overheating under load is a flagged issue and usually starts with the impeller, thermostat or fouled heat exchanger. These are the cheap parts that prevent the expensive chapter - and the engine is the 110hp D3, so source parts accordingly, not for the wrongly-cited 144hp.

provisioning_specific.item: Onan 7.5kW generator capacitor, brush and raw-water impeller set
provisioning_specific.why: The 220V watermaker, Climma AC and battery charging all lean on the generator, and generator neglect is common on brokerage boats. Capacitor and brush failure are the typical Onan faults and are not stocked in remote anchorages.

provisioning_specific.item: Mastervolt inverter spare fuses and contactor relay
provisioning_specific.why: The whole 220V system - watermaker, AC, galley - runs through the dual Mastervolt inverters. Their fuses and contactors are the highest-probability failure point and are not available outside major hubs.

provisioning_specific.item: Dessalator watermaker pre-filter cartridges (12-month supply) and membrane spare
provisioning_specific.why: The 240-250 L/h Dessalator plus 800L tank is what makes the boat self-sufficient for a couple. Pre-filter clogging is the leading cause of lost output and membrane damage, and the cartridge sizes are not stocked in most anchorages.

provisioning_specific.item: Climma reverse-cycle AC/heat filters and refrigerant top-up access
provisioning_specific.why: Climma units provide both the cabin heating that supports cold-weather cruising and the cooling for the tropics. Clogged filters are the commonest cause of weak output and are a simple self-service item; keep a filter set aboard.

provisioning_specific.item: Manual furler override service kit (override fixings, anti-seize, bearing grease)
provisioning_specific.why: The main furler's manual override works by unbolting two fixings, but those fixings and the swivel bearings seize from corrosion if never exercised. Keeping the override serviceable is the cheap insurance behind the electric furler.

provisioning_specific.item: 24V breakers and bus-bar fuse assortment (10A-80A)
provisioning_specific.why: The complex 24V system has multiple high-draw loads on a non-standard Amel bus architecture; compatible 24V breakers and fuses are not universally stocked and should be carried from departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Jon Buoy MOB module repack and jacklines/tethers to current standard
provisioning_specific.why: Recovering a casualty over the high topsides of a 24-tonne ketch shorthanded is hard, so the realistic strategy is staying aboard. Keep the MOB module within its repack interval and the cockpit-to-bow jacklines and tethers current.

gaps: stability.avs - No published Angle of Vanishing Stability for the Amel 55. General offshore guidance favours AVS above 120 degrees, but no measured figure exists for this boat.
stability.stix - No published STIX score. Category A certification implies the boat clears the regulatory threshold, but the actual number is not published.
Righting-moment / GZ curve - No stability curve data is published; the available ratios (Capsize Screening 1.75, Comfort 38.66, SA/D 15.58) are derived from dimensions, not measured stability.
self_steering.windvane_option - No factory windvane, and no source confirms a clean aftermarket servo-pendulum installation given the transom and rudder geometry.
Heavy weather storm reports - No first-hand published Amel 55 storm log was located; heavy-weather assessment rests on design characteristics and the documented furler risk.
Furler/C-drive/steering repair costs - Specialist repair figures are estimates from buyer's guides and general marine rates, not Amel-published service pricing.
Exact refrigeration and window-count figures - General standard is well regarded but specific volumes and counts varied between sources and could not be pinned down.

cleaned_facts.value: EU CE Category A (Ocean), the highest classification, designed for winds above Beaufort Force 8 and significant wave heights well above 4m. Confirmed by sailboatdata and Category A certification references. Built by Amel to a Berret-Racoupeau design from roughly 2011 to 2018.

5,900 kg cast iron L-shaped keel with bulb, 27% ballast ratio; the prop shaft runs through the keel so the folding prop sits at the keel's trailing edge forward of the rudder. Boat-specs and the boats.com test confirm the keel weight, ratio and the keel-integrated driveline.

Capsize Screening Formula 1.75, Comfort Ratio 38.66, SA/D 15.58 per SailWiki. These are dimension-derived ratios, not measured stability data: the low SA/D matches the boat's deliberately easygoing performance and the Comfort Ratio confirms its moderate-to-heavy bluewater character. No AVS or STIX is published.

Hydronic diesel furnace for distributed heating plus optional Climma reverse-cycle air conditioning/heat. Confirmed on the C'LUV listing and consistent with the Yachting Monthly description of standard heating in the liveaboard package.

Both masts are deck-stepped onto laminated compression pillars, with chainplate tie-rods bonded into the hull and two swept-back spreader levels. Confirmed by boat-specs and the TradeABoat review. No keel-stepped cold bridge through the cabin.

Four watertight bulkheads divide the hull (boats.com test), separating accommodation from bow locker and lazarette. The earlier '5 compartments / 2 bulkheads' figure was sourced from the different Amel 50 and has been corrected to four.

Deep sheltered centre cockpit enclosed by Cabriolet canvas or GRP hardtop; sail handling is from the helm via electric controls so the crew stays under cover. This is an enclosed cockpit, not a separate wheelhouse with a wheel - the earlier 'enclosed wheelhouse with leather-covered wheel' phrasing was corrected.
Enclosed Cabriolet canvas or GRP hardtop cockpit; the hardtop is the recommended world-cruising option for permanent shade with no canvas maintenance. Confirmed by TradeABoat.
Dessalator watermaker standard at roughly 240-250 L/h per the boats.com test and C'LUV listing (the earlier 160 L/h figure was wrong). 220V powered, paired with 800L water tankage.
900 litres fuel and 800 litres water confirmed by TradeABoat and spec databases. Long motoring range from the economical D3-110 plus watermaker autonomy for a couple.

Hydraulic Furuno Navpilot 711 C standard, often twin units for redundancy on the better hulls. Highly automated electric sail handling - the boat spends almost all its time on autopilot. Confirmed by the C'LUV and Asia Yachting listings and the Yachting Monthly test.

No factory windvane and no mechanical self-steering fallback - the boat is autopilot-only. Aftermarket servo-pendulum fitment is theoretical and unconfirmed for this transom. Low confidence; no source addresses a clean installation.

24V DC / 220V AC system, ~600Ah at 24V house bank, Mastervolt 2500W and 2000W inverters, Onan 7.5kW generator, 400W+ solar. Confirmed on C'LUV and Asia Yachting. Enough for offshore cruising with watermaker and electronics, but the heavy AC/refrigeration load means regular generator use.

Confirmed weak astern handling: the keel-trailing-edge prop sits well forward of the rudder, so there is no prop-walk but the boat slews off line going astern. Standard practice is to centre the rudder and steer on throttle and the 12hp retractable bow thruster, which is treated as essential. Confirmed by boats.com and Yachting Monthly tests.

Volvo Penta D3-110: five-cylinder, 2.4L, 110hp common-rail diesel with keel-integrated shaftdrive, in a walk-in engine room under the cockpit sole. The earlier 144hp figure was wrong and is corrected to 110hp per boats.com and TradeABoat.
Large swim/folding platform, cavernous lazarette, self-bailing cockpit. Built for tender and water access rather than stern MOB recovery. Confirmed by TradeABoat.

Jon Buoy MOB module documented on C'LUV; solid bow-to-stern stainless guardrails support staying aboard. Over-the-side recovery of a casualty on a 24-tonne ketch shorthanded is realistically difficult.

Cutter-headed ketch: mainsail ~47 m², genoa ~47 m², mizzen ~29 m², staysail option 22 m², all electric-furling with manual overrides. The split rig means no single sail is unmanageable and the boat reduces progressively to mizzen-and-staysail in heavy weather. Genoa corrected to ~47 m² (the earlier 75 m² was wrong). Confirmed by boats.com and Yachting Monthly.

Confirmed by buyer's guides and the owner community: (1) electric furler hesitation/jamming - safety-critical and expensive, with manual overrides that seize if neglected; (2) hidden steering U-joint wear needing cockpit disassembly; (3) balsa deck-core moisture - a potential six-figure problem; (4) rudder play and skeg cracking; (5) Volvo D3 overheating under load; (6) C-drive seawater-into-oil and documented improvised foam-seal repairs; (7) costly winch/generator/electrical neglect.

sources.title: Amel 55 Review - What Buyers Should Inspect Before a Survey - TrueNorth Yacht Advisors

cleaned_facts.value: Used Amel 55s (roughly 2011-2017) trade about $540k-$865k (620k-790k euros), with a 2012 around $750k and a 2015 around $835k as representative asks. Condition drives the spread: a documented boat sits high, a neglected one can need a six-figure refit. From boats.com / YachtWorld brokerage data.

Volvo D3-110, Onan 7.5kW generator and Furuno electronics are mainstream and well supported; Amel minimised through-hulls with a single seawater inlet/manifold. Amel-specific items (Reckmann furlers, C-drive, bespoke 24V architecture) need specialist dealers but are backed by the 1,000+ member Amel Yacht Owners community and Amel Yacht School. The previously cited Wilkinson Marine dealer source could not be verified.

removed.reason: Original claim of 'high-density insulating foam with interior coating' and 'very efficient sound insulation in the engine room' was not supported by the cited sales listing. Replaced with a sourced, lower-confidence fact noting solid GRP below the waterline and a balsa-cored deck (per the boats.com test), which also flags the real moisture-ingress risk.

Original specifics ('eight hull side windows', 'centerline window opens', 'electric galley extractor hood') were sourced to a dead swiftsureyachts.com/amel-55-vivace/ link (404). Retained as a general, lower-confidence ventilation fact sourced to the Yachting Monthly review rather than the dead link.

fabrication_flags: Engine horsepower: original record stated 'Volvo Penta 144 HP 5-cylinder.' Corrected to Volvo Penta D3-110 (110hp) - confirmed independently by boats.com and TradeABoat. The 144hp figure was fabricated.
Generator capacity: original 'Onan 11kVa' corrected to Onan 7.5kW, confirmed by the boats.com test and the Asia Yachting 2015 listing.
Watermaker capacity: original '160 L/h' corrected to ~240-250 L/h Dessalator, confirmed by the boats.com test and C'LUV listing.
Genoa sail area: original '75 m²' corrected to ~47 m² per the boats.com test, which lists mainsail and genoa both at 47 m².

Watertight bulkheads: original '5 watertight compartments with 2 interior watertight bulkheads' was sourced from the Amel 50, a different model. Corrected to four watertight bulkheads per the boats.com Amel 55 test.

Inside helm: original 'enclosed wheelhouse with leather-covered stainless steel wheel' overstated the layout. The boat has a sheltered enclosed centre cockpit (Cabriolet or GRP hardtop), not a separate wheelhouse.
Battery variant: original '8 x 640 Ah AGM' has no source support; the confirmed bank is ~600Ah at 24V (six 200Ah batteries).
Wilkinson Marine dealer link: could not be verified as an Amel 55 parts/dealer source; removed from sourcing.
verify.verdict: Credible after correction. The structural and systems picture is now well supported by magazine tests (boats.com, Yachting Monthly), the TradeABoat review, the Amel Yacht School buying guide, brokerage listings and the Amel Yacht Owners community. Several invented or mis-sourced numbers from the first pass have been corrected against multiple sources: the engine is the 110hp Volvo D3, not 144hp; the generator is 7.5kW, not 11kVA; the watermaker is ~240-250 L/h, not 160; the genoa is ~47 m², not 75; there are four watertight bulkheads, not five-compartments/two-bulkheads; and the 'enclosed wheelhouse with a wheel' is really a sheltered enclosed centre cockpit. The boat's two defining honest weaknesses - safety-critical electric furlers and notably weak astern handling - are now documented from named sources rather than inferred. No AVS or STIX is published, and that is stated plainly. The remaining soft spots are the absence of a first-hand storm log and the reliance on buyer's-guide estimates for Amel-specific repair costs.

cold_score.rationale: The Amel 55 is a genuinely strong cold-weather boat. It has full distributed heating from a hydronic diesel furnace backed by optional Climma reverse-cycle heat, and a deeply sheltered centre cockpit that can be fully enclosed by a GRP hardtop - so the crew steers and handles sail from under cover in cold, wet conditions, which is exactly what matters offshore in high latitudes. The masts are deck-stepped rather than keel-stepped, so there is no cold bridge driving condensation down through the cabin. The hull is solid GRP below the waterline and the boat is well insulated and sound-deadened. The score is held below the top band only because exact insulation figures are not published and the balsa-cored deck is a moisture liability if fittings are neglected, not because of any real cold-weather shortcoming.
cold_score.basis: Heating: C'LUV listing and Yachting Monthly. Enclosed hardtop cockpit and deck-stepped masts: TradeABoat and Yachting Monthly. Insulation and deck core: boats.com test.

tropic_score.rationale: The boat is built for tropical liveaboard cruising. The enclosed cockpit - especially the GRP hardtop - gives permanent helm and cockpit shade, the standard Dessalator watermaker (around 240-250 litres an hour) plus 800 litres of tankage make fresh water effectively unlimited for a couple, and cold storage is generous with multiple fridges and freezer capacity. Ventilation is good through hatches, hull windows and the openable cockpit, with optional Climma air conditioning for active cooling. The only real caveats are that the heavy refrigeration and air-conditioning load leans hard on the generator and battery bank, and the boat's deliberately low sail-area-to-displacement ratio means it is no flyer in the light airs of the trades - it will motor-sail more than a livelier boat. Strong tropical scoring on shade, water and provisioning, trimmed slightly for the power-hungry comfort systems and modest light-air performance.
tropic_score.basis: Shade: TradeABoat hardtop. Watermaker ~240-250 L/h and 800L water: boats.com and C'LUV. Refrigeration: C'LUV. Light-air SA/D 15.58: SailWiki. AC power draw: Yachting Monthly.

stability_score.rationale: No measured stability number - AVS, STIX or righting-moment curve - is published for the Amel 55, so the score rests on what is confirmed: EU Category A (Ocean) certification, a 27% ballast ratio on a 5,900 kg cast-iron keel, a heavy 24-tonne displacement, and dimension-derived ratios that all point the right way (Capsize Screening 1.75, comfortably under the 2.0 offshore threshold, and a Comfort Ratio of 38.66 in the moderate-to-heavy bluewater band). Category A is the rubric anchor for a value of 80. Confidence is held to the mid-range precisely because there is no published numeric stability datum to confirm the picture - the rating reflects a boat designed and certified for ocean work, not a measured stability curve.
stability_score.basis: Category A confirmed: sailboatdata and ecys.com. Ballast/displacement: boat-specs.com. Capsize and comfort ratios: SailWiki. AVS and STIX not published in any source.

storm_verdict.text: There is no first-hand published storm log specific to the Amel 55, so this is an assessment of design rather than a performance record. The boat is built for heavy weather: Category A certification, a heavy 24-tonne hull with a 27% ballast ratio, four watertight bulkheads, a deeply sheltered enclosed cockpit that keeps the crew protected, and a cutter-headed ketch rig that can be wound down progressively to a reefed mizzen and staysail alone when it really blows. The split rig means no single sail is unmanageable. The honest catch is that all of that heavy-weather security runs through the boat's electric furlers, and a furler that hesitates or jams as a gale builds is the single most dangerous failure mode on this boat - the buyer's guides and the owner community both flag it as safety-critical, and the manual overrides seize from corrosion if they are never exercised. The C-drive seal issue is a flooding risk worth taking seriously under sustained offshore stress, and the weak astern handling matters in a crowded storm-bound anchorage. In short: a fundamentally capable, comfortable heavy-weather boat whose real storm risk is mechanical reliability of the furling and drive systems, not the hull or rig design itself.

by_item.note: Deck-stepped ketch with two masts carries roughly 40% more standing rigging than a comparable sloop. The oldest hulls (around 2011) are past the 10-15 year offshore re-rig interval and the youngest (around 2018) are approaching it, so most boats need or soon need a re-rig plus a rig pull to inspect tangs, chainplates and mast bases.

Full four-sail furling suit. The areas are modest individually (main and genoa ~47 m² each, mizzen ~29 m², staysail 22 m²) but a premium attaches to furling-compatible profiles that work with the Reckmann systems. Use the higher end if any sail is original to the boat.

The Amel 55's signature known issue and a safety-critical one. Covers motor and gearbox overhaul or replacement on the in-mast main and the genoa furler, plus freeing and servicing the manual overrides so they actually work in an emergency. Inspect under load on the sea trial - hesitation or stiffness means budget the upper end.

Confirmed common failure. U-joints wear through hidden under the enclosed cockpit and announce themselves only as stiffness or a metallic grinding. Access requires cockpit disassembly, which drives the labour cost. Inspect carefully at survey.

Seawater mixing into the C-drive oil is documented with photographic evidence. Seal kit plus specialist labour; the upper end applies if a previous improvised foam-sealed repair (also documented) has to be properly remediated with correct hardware.

The engine is the 110hp Volvo D3, confirmed by boats.com and TradeABoat. Most examples need comprehensive service - impeller, heat exchanger, zincs, thermostat, injectors, full fluids - rather than a repower. Chronic overheating under load is the warning sign of a costlier chapter; add a repower budget only if hours are very high or overheating is documented.

Generator confirmed at 7.5kW. Capacitors, brushes, impeller and cooling-circuit service. Onan parts are widely available, but generator neglect is common on brokerage boats and the boat leans on it for AC, watermaker and charging.

The balsa-cored deck and coachroof can take on moisture around leaking fittings, and the buyer's guides flag this as a potential six-figure problem. The low end is localised epoxy treatment of isolated wet spots; the high end is full coachroof core replacement. A moisture-meter survey of the whole deck is mandatory, and this single item can exceed every other one on the list if damage is extensive.

The ~600Ah-at-24V bank (six 200Ah batteries) is past its AGM service life on every hull by 2026. Lower end for a like-for-like AGM replacement; upper end for a LiFePO4 upgrade, which is cost-effective here because the Mastervolt inverter infrastructure is already fitted and lithium cuts generator hours.

Service the electric primary and sheet winches and inspect the complex 24V wiring, breakers, bus bars and inverter/charger contactors. Excellent when maintained and expensive once neglected - the system rewards a boat with documented service history and punishes one without.
Rudder bearing replacement if play is detected and skeg crack repair by epoxy injection or rebuild. Check the encapsulated keel for grounding history at the same time.

Amel's single seawater inlet and manifold keeps the through-hull count low, but the fittings present should be pressure-tested and partially replaced at this age. Flagged as a survey priority by the Amel Yacht School guide.

refit_projection.assumptions: Production years are taken as roughly 2011-2018, putting hulls at 8 to 15 years old in 2026. Engine corrected to the 110hp Volvo D3 and generator to 7.5kW Onan; the earlier 144hp and 11kVA figures are ignored. Every range reflects condition-contingent spend rather than a fixed bill: a documented, well-serviced boat lands in the lower half of each item, while a neglected brokerage example can reach or exceed the upper bounds - the Amel Yacht School's own guidance pegs a near-perfect boat at $6,000-$10,000 of immediate work, an average one at $15,000-$20,000 (sometimes over $50,000), and a below-average one at $50,000-$100,000-plus, which brackets these totals well. The deck-core item is conditional: zero if the survey finds no moisture, potentially beyond the stated upper bound if it is extensive. Items are additive worst-case; a single recent, well-kept hull may need only a subset. All figures are USD 2026 and exclude a full repower, which should be added only if engine hours are very high or chronic overheating is documented.

provisioning_specific.item: Reckmann electric furler motor brushes, gearbox seals and grease (both masts)
provisioning_specific.why: Electric furling hesitation is the boat's most dangerous and expensive known failure, and a furler that will not furl in a rising gale is an offshore emergency. Carry brushes, seals and the correct lubricant for both the in-mast main and the genoa Reckmann units, and keep the manual overrides exercised so they free off when you need them.

provisioning_specific.item: C-drive mechanical seal kit and spare C-drive oil
provisioning_specific.why: Seawater mixing into the C-drive oil is documented with photographic evidence. A seal failure offshore needs immediate attention, and carrying the seal kit and correct oil grade avoids a haul-out diversion in a remote port.

provisioning_specific.item: Steering U-joint replacement assembly
provisioning_specific.why: U-joint wear is a common failure that hides under the enclosed cockpit and shows up only as stiffness or grinding. A spare assembly turns an offshore failure into an at-anchor repair instead of a tow.

provisioning_specific.item: Hydraulic fluid, filters and bleed kit for the Furuno autopilot circuit
provisioning_specific.why: The boat is autopilot-only with no windvane fallback, and the twin Furuno units share a hydraulic circuit, so a leak or pump fault can disable both. Carry fluid and know the bleed procedure to keep steering automated on long shorthanded passages.

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo Penta D3-110 raw-water impeller, thermostat and heat-exchanger zinc kit
provisioning_specific.why: Overheating under load is a flagged issue that usually starts with the impeller, thermostat or a fouled heat exchanger - the cheap parts that head off the expensive chapter. Source for the 110hp D3, not the wrongly-cited 144hp.

provisioning_specific.item: Onan 7.5kW generator capacitor, brush and raw-water impeller set
provisioning_specific.why: The 220V watermaker, Climma AC and battery charging all depend on the generator, and generator neglect is common on brokerage boats. Capacitor and brush failures are the typical Onan faults and are not stocked in remote anchorages.

provisioning_specific.item: Mastervolt inverter spare fuses and contactor relay
provisioning_specific.why: The whole 220V system - watermaker, AC, galley - runs through the dual Mastervolt inverters, and their fuses and contactors are the highest-probability failure point. They are not available outside major hubs.

provisioning_specific.item: Dessalator watermaker pre-filter cartridges (12-month supply) and membrane spare
provisioning_specific.why: The 240-250 L/h Dessalator plus the 800L tank is what makes the boat self-sufficient for a couple. Pre-filter clogging is the leading cause of lost output and membrane damage, and the cartridge sizes are hard to find away from major cruising hubs.

provisioning_specific.item: Climma reverse-cycle AC/heat filters and refrigerant top-up access
provisioning_specific.why: The Climma units deliver both the cabin heating that supports cold-weather cruising and the cooling for the tropics. Clogged filters are the commonest cause of weak output and are a simple self-service item, so keep a filter set aboard.

provisioning_specific.item: Manual furler override service kit (override fixings, anti-seize, bearing grease)
provisioning_specific.why: The main furler's manual override works by unbolting two fixings, but those fixings and the swivel bearings seize from corrosion if they are never exercised. Keeping the override serviceable is the cheap insurance behind the electric furler.

provisioning_specific.item: 24V breakers and bus-bar fuse assortment (10A-80A)
provisioning_specific.why: The complex 24V system runs multiple high-draw loads on a non-standard Amel bus architecture; compatible 24V breakers and fuses are not universally stocked and should be carried from departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Jon Buoy MOB module repack and jacklines/tethers to current standard
provisioning_specific.why: Recovering a casualty over the high topsides of a 24-tonne ketch shorthanded is genuinely hard, so the realistic strategy is staying aboard. Keep the MOB module within its repack interval and the cockpit-to-bow jacklines and tethers current.

field_reports.title: All sails set from the cockpit in five minutes
field_reports.account: Reviewing the ketch-rigged Amel 55 off Hyères on the Côte d'Azur, a tester deployed the full sail plan — genoa, staysail, main and mizzen, 145 m² in all — in about five minutes using joystick controls from the cockpit. In light 8.5-knot air the boat made 4.2 knots hard on the wind at roughly 35 degrees; the reviewer judged it would be 'right at home' running in the Atlantic trades under twin headsails rather than excelling in flat light air.
field_reports.conditions: 8.5 knots true, ~4.2 knots boatspeed close-hauled at ~35 degrees

field_reports.title: Heavy helm when over-canvassed
field_reports.account: Yachting Monthly's Chris Beeson found the Amel 55 comfortable and unhurried rather than quick, in keeping with its 30-tonne-plus displacement. He noted the helm went 'very heavy' when the boat was over-canvassed, particularly with too much mizzen set — a handling caution for owners learning to balance the ketch rig.

field_reports.title: A solid, fast ketch built ready to cross oceans
field_reports.account: Blue Water Sailing described the Amel 55 as 'a solid and fast ketch with true blue water capabilities,' aimed at mature couples seeking self-sufficiency, with capacity to cruise comfortably with a second couple aboard. The magazine noted boats arrive fully outfitted from the yard with Amel-tested sails, generator, watermaker and gear, reflecting the 'ready-to-cruise' philosophy and an even spread of canvas so no single sail is unmanageable on passage.

field_reports.title: Watertight bulkheads and skeg-hung rudder
field_reports.account: The Amel 55's offshore credentials per the TradeABoat sea trial include four watertight bulkheads, a balsa-sandwich hull with solid GRP below the waterline, a skeg-protected rudder integrated with the cast-iron keel, and a propeller set in the keel's trailing edge to eliminate propwalk. The enclosed cockpit uses 12mm Plexiglas screens and a canvas Cabriolet top, and the molded non-slip deck removes teak upkeep — all geared to secure, low-maintenance ocean cruising.

field_reports.title: Amel 55s stage for Atlantic crossings in the Canaries
field_reports.account: Circumnavigators Judy and Bill (SV BeBe, a Super Maramu) documented an unusually large gathering of Amels at Marina Lanzarote in November 2015 as boats staged for Atlantic crossings. Among them were the Amel 55s Kandiba (Turkish owners Hassan and Zehrya, taking on a niece as crew for the crossing) and Rainbow (Hamburg-flagged), alongside an Amel 54, Now or Ever — a snapshot of the active 55-era owner community using the Canaries as a jumping-off point.

source.title: S/V BeBe: Lots of Amels in the Canary Islands! (owner blog)

field_reports.title: Buyers smitten at the boat show, then reconsider
field_reports.account: Prospective circumnavigators Glen and Oana (Sail Cloudy Bay) were 'smitten' by the Amel 55 at the 2016 Düsseldorf show — Glen citing the deck-accessed engine room, ketch rig, protected nav station and watertight bulkheads; Oana the modern looks and sundeck. They ultimately bought a Hallberg-Rassy 54 instead, partly on cost (a roughly 980k euro show offer would have left no cruising kitty) and partly because, seen on the water, the 55s struck them as 'square and blocky.' A useful, honest non-owner perspective on the 55's trade-offs.

source.title: How It Started, with Amel 55 — Sail Cloudy Bay (blog)
